point_001: The claim that DART requires 'no external parameters' is questionable, since training new token embeddings (for prompts and labels) still introduces trainable parameters, even if they reuse slots from pre-existing unused/special tokens in the vocabulary.
point_002: The number of template/prompt tokens to use is not well justified, and no ablation study systematically explores how performance varies with different token counts.
point_003: The notation and description in Section 4 are not immediately intuitive and require multiple readings; Figure 1 could be expanded to aid comprehension.
point_004: The Fluency Constraint Objective is based on MLM, yet GPT-2-medium (an autoregressive model) is used in Section 5.5 and Figure 2b without explaining how this objective applies to a non-MLM model.
point_005: On the 10 popular benchmark datasets, DART's improvements over LM-BFF are marginal for most tasks (only SST-2 and MR show notable gains), making it difficult to conclude that differentiable prompts consistently outperform fixed/static prompts; this discrepancy with the stronger gains on relation/event extraction datasets is not adequately discussed.
point_006: The ablation study lacks specific numerical results, limiting the reader's ability to quantitatively assess the contribution of each component.
point_007: Comparisons with related soft-prompt methods such as Prefix-Tuning and WARP are absent from the experiments.
point_008: Performance of DART with the full training set (not just few-shot) on GLUE tasks is not reported, making it harder to contextualize the few-shot results.
point_009: Training curves in Figures 3 and 4 do not indicate the number of training iterations or epochs, reducing reproducibility and interpretability.
point_010: It is unclear how the method generalizes beyond classification tasks (e.g., structured prediction tasks like constituency or dependency parsing), limiting the scope of the contribution.
point_011: The paper does not explore constraining the optimized prompt tokens to lie within the existing vocabulary (analogous to AutoPrompt), which would be a natural baseline to compare against.
point_012: Section 4.3's explanation of how label representations are learned is unclear—specifically whether they are learned from scratch and, if so, how this differs from standard fine-tuning.
